content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: There is ___ in the bag — it is completely empty.
distractor_rationale: anything requires a negative auxiliary (e.g. there isn't anything); used alone without negation it is wrong here
something implies the bag contains an item, which contradicts 'completely empty'
everything implies the bag is full of items, directly contradicting 'completely empty'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Is ___ home? I knocked three times but nobody answered the door.
distractor_rationale: someone is used in affirmative statements and positive expectations, not in open questions where the answer is unknown
no one makes the question self-contradictory — you would not ask if no one is home and then say nobody answered
everyone implies all people are home, which is contradicted by the fact that nobody answered

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ in the class passed the test — the teacher was very happy with the results.
distractor_rationale: anyone in a statement means 'any person at all' and does not convey that all students passed, so it does not explain the teacher's happiness
someone means only one person passed, which would not make the teacher very happy with the overall results
no one means nobody passed, which directly contradicts the teacher being very happy

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I looked ___ for my phone — on the sofa, in my bag, and on the kitchen table — but I still couldn't find it.
distractor_rationale: somewhere means one unspecified location, which contradicts the list of multiple places searched
anywhere requires a negative or question context; in an affirmative statement it is ungrammatical without negation
nowhere creates a double negative with 'couldn't', making the sentence mean the speaker found the phone, which contradicts the context

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The doctor said I shouldn't eat ___ spicy for the next two weeks because of my stomach.
distractor_rationale: something is used in affirmative contexts or when a positive answer is expected; after 'shouldn't' it produces an ungrammatical double-meaning
nothing creates a double negative with 'shouldn't', meaning the doctor said I should eat spicy food, which contradicts the medical advice
everything means I shouldn't eat all spicy things individually listed, but the natural meaning here is 'any spicy food at all', and everything is semantically wrong in this negative restriction

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Everyone in the office ___ working late tonight because the deadline is tomorrow morning.
distractor_rationale: everyone is a singular indefinite pronoun and always takes a singular verb; 'are' is the most common learner error caused by the plural sense of 'everyone'
were is past tense, but the sentence refers to tonight's situation and tomorrow's deadline, making past tense contradictory
have been is present perfect continuous, which describes a completed or ongoing past action, not a plan for tonight

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: In many countries, ___ say that it is rude to arrive at a dinner party exactly on time.
distractor_rationale: one is grammatically possible but is a formal, literary register choice; the sentence uses 'say' in a colloquial reporting structure where impersonal 'they' is the standard natural form
you is the impersonal pronoun for general advice directed at the listener; here the statement reports a social norm attributed to an unnamed group, making 'they' the correct impersonal form
people is a noun, not a pronoun, and the instruction asks for a pronoun to complete the gap; inserting a noun where a subject pronoun is needed changes the grammatical category required

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Although the manager announced the new schedule yesterday, ___ in the team seems to have read it, judging by all the confused questions this morning.
distractor_rationale: anyone in an affirmative clause means 'any person at all' and does not convey the negative meaning required by the context of confused questions showing ignorance of the schedule
someone means only one person read it, which contradicts the implication that the whole team is confused and asking questions
everyone means all team members read the schedule, which directly contradicts the evidence of confused questions from all of them

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If ___ wants to join the photography club, they should add their name to the list on the notice board by Friday.
distractor_rationale: someone implies a specific unnamed person is expected to join, removing the open invitation meaning; the conditional 'if' clause signals an open hypothetical for any potential member
no one makes the conditional self-defeating — it would mean the club is not accepting new members, contradicting the instruction to sign up
everyone implies all people must join, which is not an open invitation but a compulsory requirement, changing the meaning entirely

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: When ___ moves to a new city alone, it can take several months before the place starts to feel like home.
distractor_rationale: they as impersonal subject normally refers to an unnamed external group reporting norms or facts; it does not naturally include the speaker or listener in a shared general experience the way 'you' does in conversational register
one is grammatically correct but belongs to a formal or written register; this sentence has a conversational, empathetic tone that calls for the informal impersonal 'you'
someone refers to a single unspecified individual rather than making a general statement that applies to all people in that situation, so it weakens the universal meaning of the sentence

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Is ___ home? I knocked three times but the door didn't open.
distractor_rationale: someone is used in affirmative statements or offers, not in open questions where we do not know if a person is present
everyone implies all people are home, which contradicts the unanswered door and makes the question illogical
no one is a subject that would require a statement structure, not a question, and would mean the speaker already knows nobody is there

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The fridge is empty — there is ___ to eat for breakfast.
distractor_rationale: anything requires a negative auxiliary (there isn't anything); using it after the affirmative 'there is' produces a double-negative error
something means food is present, which directly contradicts 'the fridge is empty'
everything means all food is present, which is the opposite of the empty-fridge context

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ in the meeting room agreed that the new schedule was a good idea, so the manager confirmed it immediately.
distractor_rationale: anyone in a statement without a negative or conditional context implies uncertainty about whether a person is present, and does not convey unanimous agreement
someone means only one unspecified person agreed, which does not justify the manager confirming the plan for the whole group
they refers to a previously identified group and cannot introduce a general statement about all people in the room without a prior reference

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I searched ___ in the flat for my passport — under the bed, in every drawer, and behind the sofa — but I still couldn't find it.
distractor_rationale: somewhere means only one unspecified place was searched, which contradicts the list of multiple locations and the emphatic tone of the sentence
anywhere requires a negative or question context; in an affirmative clause it does not convey the meaning of searching all possible places
nowhere means no place was searched, which directly contradicts the list of locations given and requires a negative verb structure

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: In many countries, ___ say that if you work hard and stay consistent, you can achieve almost anything you set your mind to.
distractor_rationale: one is possible in very formal written English but is not the natural choice for reporting a widely held popular belief in everyday speech; the sentence's informal register and the plural verb 'say' make 'they' the correct impersonal subject here
someone refers to a single unidentified individual, but the plural verb 'say' and the context of a widespread cultural belief require a plural impersonal subject

anyone in an affirmative statement without a conditional or negative context does not function as an impersonal subject meaning 'people in general'; it would require a conditional or negative frame to be grammatical

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: There is ___ in the kitchen — I can hear voices.
distractor_rationale: anyone is used in questions and negatives, not in affirmative statements about a specific presence
no one contradicts the evidence of voices being heard
everyone implies all possible people are present, which is not supported by the context

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: I looked in every drawer but I couldn't find ___ .
distractor_rationale: something is used in affirmative contexts; after a negative verb (couldn't), anything is required
nothing creates a double negative with couldn't, making the sentence grammatically incorrect
everything contradicts the meaning — the speaker found no items at all, not all items

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ in the class passed the test — the teacher was very pleased.
distractor_rationale: anyone refers to any individual in a question or negative context, not to the whole group in an affirmative result
someone refers to an unspecified individual, not to the entire class
no one contradicts the teacher being pleased and the positive outcome described

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Has ___ called for me while I was out this afternoon?
distractor_rationale: someone is used in affirmative sentences or polite requests, not in direct questions asking about an unknown caller
no one would make the question a statement of fact rather than a genuine inquiry
everyone implies all people called, which is implausible and contradicts the open question

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The fridge is completely empty — there is ___ to eat in the house.
distractor_rationale: anything requires a negative auxiliary (e.g. there isn't); with the affirmative there is, nothing is needed
something means food is available, which directly contradicts the empty fridge
everything means all food is present, which is the opposite of what the context describes

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: In many countries, ___ drive on the left side of the road, which surprises visitors from elsewhere.
distractor_rationale: someone refers to a single unidentified individual, not to a general social practice in a country
anyone is used in questions or negative contexts and cannot express a general impersonal rule
everyone would mean every single person drives on the left, which is factually inaccurate as a general statement about a country's rule

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Everyone on the team ___ responsible for submitting their report by Friday.
distractor_rationale: are is the plural form; everyone always takes a singular verb even though it refers to a group
were is past tense and plural; the sentence describes a present obligation, not a past one
have been is present perfect plural; everyone requires a singular verb and the context calls for simple present

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: She searched ___ for her passport before the flight but couldn't find it.
distractor_rationale: somewhere indicates one unspecified location, which contradicts the exhaustive searching implied by the context
anywhere requires a negative auxiliary or question context; with the affirmative searched, it is not standard
nowhere creates a double negative with couldn't and means she had no place to search, which contradicts the active searching described

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: When ___ works hard and stays focused, the results usually speak for themselves over time.
distractor_rationale: you is also possible in informal impersonal statements but in this formal, balanced sentence one is the register-appropriate impersonal pronoun that the context signals through its elevated phrasing
someone refers to a specific unknown individual, not to people in general as a universal truth
anyone is used in conditional or negative contexts and does not express a general impersonal truth in affirmative statements

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The manager announced that ___ had taken the missing files, so the whole department had to stay late.
distractor_rationale: anyone is used in questions and negatives; in this affirmative reported statement about a real but unknown person, someone is required
no one means nobody took the files, which contradicts the consequence of the whole department staying late as a result of the theft
everyone means all staff took the files, which is implausible and contradicts the singular unknown perpetrator implied by the announcement

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I don't know nothing about the new timetable.
content.error_sentence: I don't know nothing about the new timetable.
content.corrected_sentence: I don't know anything about the new timetable.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Everyone are happy about the school trip next week.
content.error_sentence: Everyone are happy about the school trip next week.
content.corrected_sentence: Everyone is happy about the school trip next week.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Is there someone waiting outside the office right now?
content.error_sentence: Is there someone waiting outside the office right now?
content.corrected_sentence: Is there anyone waiting outside the office right now?

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She couldn't find her keys somewhere in the flat, so she was late for work.
content.error_sentence: She couldn't find her keys somewhere in the flat, so she was late for work.
content.corrected_sentence: She couldn't find her keys anywhere in the flat, so she was late for work.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Nobody didn't call the manager during the meeting, so the problem was not solved.
content.error_sentence: Nobody didn't call the manager during the meeting, so the problem was not solved.
content.corrected_sentence: Nobody called the manager during the meeting, so the problem was not solved.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: In many countries, you has to carry an identity card at all times.
content.error_sentence: In many countries, you has to carry an identity card at all times.
content.corrected_sentence: In many countries, you have to carry an identity card at all times.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: They say that one should always check their work carefully before one submits them.
content.error_sentence: They say that one should always check their work carefully before one submits them.
content.corrected_sentence: They say that one should always check their work carefully before one submits it.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Although the manager asked for feedback, no one of the employees said something critical about the new policy.
content.error_sentence: Although the manager asked for feedback, no one of the employees said something critical about the new policy.
content.corrected_sentence: Although the manager asked for feedback, no one of the employees said anything critical about the new policy.
